claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 6 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 6 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child in care for 7 weeks (within 8-week limit)
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 6 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child in care for 7 weeks (within 8-week limit); Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child in care for 7 weeks (within 8-week limit)
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start by gathering some information about your situation.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
To assess your eligibility for Child Benefit, I'll need to ask you some questions. Child Benefit eligibility is assessed separately for each child.

First, let me ask: **Do you have a child or children that you are responsible for?** If yes, could you tell me how many children you'd like to check eligibility for?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have one child I'd like to check eligibility for. Their name is Alex, and they're 6 years old and live with me here in the UK.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! Since Alex is 6 years old and lives with you in the UK, that's helpful to know.

Now I need to ask a few more questions to complete the assessment:

**Are you responsible for Alex?** By this I mean, do you have parental responsibility or are you the main person caring for them?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, absolutely. I'm their parent and I'm responsible for them. They live with me full-time.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for confirming that. Now I need to ask:

**Are you or your partner currently getting certain benefits or support for Alex from the government?** Specifically, I'm asking if you're receiving any of the following for Alex:

- Universal Credit (that includes an amount for Alex)
- Income Support
- Income-based Jobseeker's Allowance
- Income-related Employment and Support Allowance
- Pension Credit
- Housing Benefit
- Working Tax Credit
- Child Tax Credit

Are you receiving any of these benefits that include an amount for Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I'm not receiving any of those benefits for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I have a few more questions to complete the assessment.

**Does Alex normally live with you?** I understand they live with you full-time, but I need to confirm they're not currently in any of these situations:

- In hospital for more than 12 weeks
- In local authority care or a care home
- Living with someone else who might also be claiming Child Benefit for them

Can you confirm Alex lives with you normally and none of these situations apply?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex lives with me normally. Actually, they were in local authority care for a little while - just a couple of months - but they're back home with me now.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for sharing that information. To make sure I understand the situation correctly, I need to ask a couple of follow-up questions about the time Alex was in local authority care:

**When Alex was in local authority care, were they coming home to stay with you for at least 2 consecutive nights each week (or averaging 2 nights per week)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, they weren't coming home during that time. They were at the care home full-time while they were there.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. Now I need to ask:

**How long ago did Alex return home from the local authority care?** In other words, when did they come back to live with you full-time?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Oh, they came back quite recently - they've only been home for about a week now.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for that information. Just to be clear about the timeline - **how long in total was Alex in local authority care?** You mentioned it was a couple of months - was it more than 8 weeks?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Let me think... yes, it was 7 weeks in total.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. That's very helpful information.

Now I need to ask about your own circumstances:

**Are you currently living or working in the UK?** (You mentioned Alex lives with you in the UK, but I need to confirm your status as well)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK. I'm here in England with Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. I have just a couple more questions to complete the assessment.

**Are you considered 'habitually resident' in the UK?** This generally means the UK is your settled home where you normally live, and you have the right to reside here (for example, you're a UK citizen, have settled status, or have the right to live and work here).

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I'm habitually resident here. The UK is my settled home and I have the right to live here.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent, thank you. Just one final question:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?** For example, another parent, family member, or anyone else?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for answering all my questions. Let me now review all the information you've provided to assess Alex's eligibility for Child Benefit.

Let me carefully consider the rules based on what you've told me:

**About Alex:**
- 6 years old (under 16)
- Lives with you normally now
- You are their parent and responsible for them
- Was in local authority care for 7 weeks (without coming home for at least 2 nights per week)
- Has been back home with you for about 1 week

**About you:**
- Living and habitually resident in the UK
- Not receiving other benefits for Alex
- No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex

According to the rules on Child Benefit for children in care, you can continue to receive Child Benefit if your child is in local authority care for **up to 8 weeks**. However, this applies if you were already receiving Child Benefit before they went into care, or if you're making a new claim.

Since Alex was in care for 7 weeks (which is within the 8-week limit) and is now back home living with you, and given that:
- Alex is under 16 (automatically qualifies as a qualifying child)
- You are responsible for Alex
- Alex normally lives with you
- You are habitually resident in the UK
- No one else is claiming for Alex

**You should be eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.**

The period Alex spent in local authority care was within the 8-week threshold, and now that Alex is back home with you full-time, you meet all the eligibility criteria. You can make a claim for Child Benefit for Alex, and depending on when you claim, you may be able to backdate your claim for the periods when Alex was living with you (including potentially before and after the care period, subject to backdating rules).

Would you like any clarification about this assessment or information about how to claim?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Thank you so much for explaining all of that! That's really helpful to know. I understand now that I'm eligible for Child Benefit for Alex. I appreciate you taking the time to go through everything with me.
